title: Why the Trump administration is betting on Maduro’s deputy | FT #shorts

transcript.text: So, what comes next in Venezuela? I'd
say at the moment, the Trump
administration appears to be laying a
bet on Madura's deputy, the vice
president, Deli Rodriguez. That might
seem surprising. After all, she was
deputy to Maduro, the man Trump removed,
and she's a person who's long been
committed to the ideals of the socialist
revolution espoused by former leader Ugo
Chavez. The reason I think is because
Trump essentially is looking for a
pragmatic solution. He wants delivery.
He wants quick results in Venezuela. He
wants quick change and he thinks that
Deli Rodriguez, the vice president, is
best placed to deliver it.
>> You have a vice president who's been
appointed by Maduro and right now she's
the vice president and she's I guess the
president. She was sworn as president
just a little while ago. She had a long
conversation with Marco and she said,
"We'll do whatever you need."
>> So, what could happen now? I think there
are four possible scenarios. One is that
Deli Rodriguez consolidates herself,
delivers some of the things the
Americans want, enough for them to leave
her in power, and manages to convince
her fellow revolutionaries in the
government that she's the least worst
option for them. The second option might
be the most optimistic of all is that
the DELC government becomes a
transition. The Americans keep the
pressure up and over time Deli Rodriguez
agrees to free and fair elections,
elections in which the Venezuelan
opposition might have a chance to come
to power. The third scenario is a much
darker one. Deli Rodriguez falls prey to
an internal coup by even harderline
factions, somebody in control of the
army, the security forces and set
themselves on a path of total collision
with the United States. And then the
final scenario, I think, is the scenario
where you have chaos. You have a power
struggle, you have a fight internally,
maybe you have some spontaneous protests
breaking out, opposition movements
trying to seize power, and you have a
free-for-all. So, which might prevail?
Well, Venezuela's at the moment in a
very fragile, unstable situation. But I
think there's a common interest at the
moment. There's a common interest among
the Chavistas in keeping the regime
preserved for now to fight another day.
And there's a common interest with the
Americans who want quick results, quick
delivery, and think Deli Rodriguez is
the person best placed to deliver it. So
that might point in favor of the first
scenario being the one that would
prevail. But I think you'd have to be a
brave person after the extraordinary
events of the last few days to make any
predictions with certainty about what
might happen in Venezuela next.
